sourceMovie.title: Scream 4
sourceMovie.whyPeopleLoveIt: Audiences latched onto Scream 4 (2011) as a horror theatrical experience: big enough for a group watch, specific enough to reward a second viewing once you know where it's headed. The horror and mystery blend is the engine: it lets the film shift registers without feeling like two different movies stitched together.

recommendations.title: Scream
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Scream (1996) belongs to the same conversation as Scream 4 — both anchor their horror in mystery emotion that doesn't let go. The dark undercurrent connects them more than any plot summary would suggest.

recommendations.title: Scream 2
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: If Scream 4's horror stayed with you, Scream 2 (1997) delivers the same sensation through an entirely different story. The mystery through-line is the real common thread between them.

recommendations.title: Scream
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Where Scream 4 excels in horror, Scream (2022) doubles down on mystery to comparable effect. The tonal similarities run deep enough that watching both back-to-back feels intentional.

recommendations.title: Summer of 84
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Summer of 84 (2018) is the natural next step after Scream 4: same horror DNA, different perspective. The dark streak and mystery elevate it well above a simple genre exercise.

recommendations.title: Psycho II
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Fans of Scream 4 who respond to its horror will find Psycho II (1983) immediately familiar. Both films share a mystery sensibility without ever feeling like carbon copies of each other.

recommendations.title: Halloween
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: There's a reason Scream 4 fans keep landing on Halloween (1978) — the horror is unmistakably related even when the plots diverge. Both films operate on the same emotional frequency, especially in their third acts.

recommendations.title: Halloween
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Halloween draws from the same horror well as Scream 4, channeling thriller in ways that feel unmistakably related. The 2018 film makes a natural companion watch — similar in spirit but distinctly its own.

recommendations.title: Child's Play
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Child's Play (1988) laid groundwork for the tone and themes that Scream 4 later expanded. If Scream 4 resonated for you, this predecessor shows where that emotional DNA began.

recommendations.title: Scream VI
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: The horror appeal of Scream 4 — that dark edge, the thriller — translates cleanly into Scream VI (2023). Different setting, same essential satisfaction.

recommendations.title: The Texas Chainsaw Massacre
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: The filmmakers behind The Texas Chainsaw Massacre (2003) clearly understood what makes horror work — the same quality that keeps Scream 4 rewatchable. Expect the same dark rhythm, executed with a fresh angle.

faq.question: What should I watch after Scream 4?
faq.answer: Try Scream, Scream 2, Scream—each shares DNA with Scream 4 while changing the setting enough to feel fresh.

faq.question: What genre is Scream 4?
faq.answer: Scream 4 (2011) is best described as Horror, Mystery. Stores and algorithms use different labels, but that mix is what fans actually recognize on a rewatch.

faq.question: Is Scream 4 part of a franchise?
faq.answer: Yes—it's part of the "Scream Collection" storyline. You can enjoy it standalone, but sequels and tie-ins build on characters and continuity established here.
